eli3: Imagine you're measuring the heights of a population that has two sub-populations — professional basketball players and general adults. Each group has a bell-curve distribution, but they overlap in the middle. A Gaussian Mixture Model says: the data was generated by K bell curves blended together. The algorithm figures out each bell curve's centre, width, and how much of the population it represents.

Unlike K-Means, which makes hard assignments ('this point belongs to cluster 3'), GMM gives soft assignments: 'this point is 70% cluster 1 and 30% cluster 2.' This makes much more sense for points that sit near a boundary.

The fitting algorithm is called Expectation-Maximisation (EM). It alternates between computing how likely each point belongs to each Gaussian (E-step) and re-estimating each Gaussian's parameters to best explain those assignments (M-step).

whenWhy.title: Soft cluster memberships (probabilistic output)
whenWhy.body: When a data point sits between two clusters, hard assignment is arbitrary. GMM outputs P(cluster k | xᵢ) for every point — a proper probability. This is valuable in fraud detection (how suspicious is this transaction?), anomaly scoring, and any downstream decision that needs confidence estimates.

whenWhy.title: Density estimation and generative modelling
whenWhy.body: GMM is a flexible density estimator: p(x) = Σₖ πₖ N(x; μₖ, Σₖ). Once fit, you can sample new data points from it, compute log-likelihoods for anomaly detection, or use it as a prior in Bayesian models.

whenWhy.title: Elliptical clusters of different sizes and shapes
whenWhy.body: By allowing each component its own full covariance matrix Σₖ, GMM captures elongated, rotated elliptical clusters that K-Means cannot. Full covariance is the most flexible; diagonal covariance is more regularised (less data needed); spherical is equivalent to K-Means.

whenWhy.title: Model selection via BIC/AIC
whenWhy.body: Unlike K-Means' heuristic elbow method, GMM supports principled model selection using BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) or AIC. Fit models for K = 1, 2, …, Kmax and choose the K minimising BIC. This grounds cluster number selection in statistical theory.

deepDiveIntro: A Gaussian Mixture Model represents data as a weighted combination of K Gaussian components. To generate a point, you first randomly pick one of the K components according to its mixing weight, then draw a sample from that component's Gaussian. The model parameters — the weights, means, and covariance matrices of each component — are estimated by maximizing the likelihood of the observed data using the EM algorithm.

EM alternates between two steps. In the E-step, you compute the probability that each data point was generated by each component, given the current parameter estimates. In the M-step, you update the parameters to best explain those probabilistic assignments. Each iteration is guaranteed to increase the log-likelihood, and the process repeats until convergence.

deepDive.heading: The GMM Model
deepDive.body: The marginal density of x is a mixture of K Gaussians. This is a universal approximator of smooth densities: with enough components it can represent any continuous distribution.
deepDive.formula: p(x) = Σₖ₌₁ᴷ πₖ · N(x; μₖ, Σₖ)

deepDive.heading: E-Step: Computing Responsibilities
deepDive.body: Given the current parameter estimates, the E-step computes for each data point the probability that it was generated by each component. This is a standard application of Bayes' theorem: the component's prior weight times its likelihood for that point, divided by the total likelihood across all components. The result is a soft membership score called the responsibility. [1]
deepDive.formula: rᵢₖ = πₖ N(xᵢ; μₖ, Σₖ) / Σⱼ πⱼ N(xᵢ; μⱼ, Σⱼ)

deepDive.heading: M-Step: Updating Parameters
deepDive.body: The M-step re-estimates each component's mean and covariance using the responsibilities computed in the E-step as weights. Points with high responsibility for a component pull that component's parameters toward them. These are simply weighted versions of the standard sample mean and covariance.
deepDive.formula: μ̂ₖ = (1/Nₖ) Σᵢ rᵢₖ xᵢ ,   Σ̂ₖ = (1/Nₖ) Σᵢ rᵢₖ (xᵢ−μ̂ₖ)(xᵢ−μ̂ₖ)ᵀ

deepDive.heading: Covariance Types and Regularisation
deepDive.body: Each component can have its own full covariance matrix, allowing elliptical clusters of any shape and orientation. Restricting to diagonal or spherical covariances reduces the number of parameters and requires less data to estimate reliably. When the model tries to collapse a component onto a single point, the variance shrinks to zero and the likelihood explodes — adding a small constant to the diagonal (a regularization term) prevents this.

deepDive.heading: Model Selection: BIC and AIC
deepDive.body: The log-likelihood always increases as you add more components, so it cannot be used directly to choose the number of components. Penalized criteria such as BIC subtract a term that grows with the number of parameters, favoring simpler models. Choose the number of components that minimizes BIC.
deepDive.formula: BIC = −2 log L̂(θ̂) + m log n

takeaways: GMM models data as a weighted sum of K Gaussians: p(x) = Σₖ πₖ N(x; μₖ, Σₖ). [1]
Fitting uses EM: E-step computes soft responsibilities rᵢₖ, M-step updates π, μ, Σ using weighted statistics. [1]
EM is guaranteed to increase log-likelihood at every step but converges to a local maximum — run multiple times with different initialisations. [1]
Choose covariance type based on data size: full for large n, diagonal or spherical for small n or many components. [1]
Use BIC to select K — it penalises model complexity and is consistent. [1]
GMM is K-Means with soft assignments: K-Means ≈ GMM with spherical covariance and hard (0/1) responsibilities. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: What is the singularity problem in GMM fitting?
edgeCases.a: If a component collapses to a single data point (μₖ = xᵢ for some i), its variance Σₖ → 0 and the likelihood → ∞. This is a degenerate solution. Fix: add a small regularisation constant to the diagonal of Σₖ (reg_covar in scikit-learn, default 1e-6), or use a Bayesian GMM with an informative prior on Σₖ. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does GMM differ from K-Means?
edgeCases.a: Three key differences: (1) Soft assignments — rᵢₖ ∈ [0,1] vs hard 0/1; (2) Flexible cluster shapes — each component has its own Σₖ allowing elliptical clusters; (3) Probabilistic interpretation — GMM is a generative model with a proper likelihood, enabling BIC model selection, density estimation, and sampling. K-Means is faster but less principled. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: How do you initialise EM for GMM?
edgeCases.a: Poor initialisation leads to bad local maxima. Common strategies: (1) run K-Means first and initialise μₖ with K-Means centroids; (2) random initialisation with multiple restarts (n_init in scikit-learn); (3) random assignment of data points to components, then compute weighted statistics. Scikit-learn uses K-Means initialisation by default. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is a Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model?
edgeCases.a: A Bayesian GMM places Dirichlet priors on the mixing weights and Normal-Wishart priors on (μₖ, Σₖ). Inference via variational EM (BayesianGaussianMixture in scikit-learn) automatically prunes unused components by driving their πₖ → 0. This allows you to set K_max large and let the model find the effective K — a form of automatic model selection. [2]

Yes. The log-likelihood log p(x) under the fitted GMM is a principled anomaly score — low log-likelihood means the point is unlikely under any component. Alternatively, a point where max_k rᵢₖ is small (soft memberships are spread across all components) is likely an outlier. Threshold the log-likelihood at a percentile of the training distribution. [1]

sources.label: Bishop — Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning, Ch. 9.2
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 8.5
